data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Fruitful, But Growth Looks Limited
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome NM Market Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (NASDAQ:SFM) has seen impressive gains in the last year with its stock doubling during the period. It traded at $165.65 as of July 18, 2025, up almost 30% year-to-date. Sprouts is an American grocery chain focusing on fresh, natural, and organic food. The company offers a curated selection of organic, plant-based, and gluten-free items in an open, farmer’s market-style layout, operating 443 stores in 24 states.

The kind of rally Sprouts has seen over the past year matters a great deal for investors, particularly at a time when the macroeconomic landscape is volatile, inflation remains sticky, and consumers are tightening their wallets. In fact, even the specialty grocer’s CEO, Jack Sinclair, sold a large number of shares on July 7th and 8th to lock in a gain.

While the company continues to post robust results and maintains confident guidance, a spate of insider selling by senior executives coupled with macro headwinds raises questions about how much upside is left. Moreover, its high valuation compared to peers is a good reason to pause on buying more. Given these factors, I recommend holding your position while closely monitoring upcoming financial results, and perhaps insider sales, over the next couple of quarters before reassessing.

A Look at Sprouts' Performance

Sprouts started 2025 on a strong note, with Q1 sales up 19% year-on-year to $2.2 billion. This was driven by an impressive 11.7% rise in comparable store sales. During the period, the grocer opened three new stores, which also performed well. Net income rose nearly 58% to $180 million from a year-ago, reflecting operational efficiency. For the full fiscal year, Sprouts projects sales growth between 12% and 14% compared with 13% last year. Comparable store sales are expected to come in between 5.5% to 7.5% for the whole year relative to 7.6% in the prior fiscal.

Operational cash flow rose to $299 million, up from $220 million a year earlier. The solid cash flow helped the company make $49 million in capital expenditure to support expansion. The company also returned $219 million to shareholders through the repurchase of 1.6 million shares.

With rising demand for organic products, the grocer is steadily increasing its footprint. In FY2024, it opened 33 stores and plans to open at least 35 new stores in the current fiscal.

Competitive Scene

As far as peers are concerned, Albertsons Companies Inc.'s (ACI) expanding organic offerings could intensify competition for Sprouts. Albertsons’ O Organics brand targets Gen Z and young millennials with greater scale and variety, and may attract price-conscious shoppers looking for more options. Sprouts also sees a market overlap with Trader Joe’s (privately held) with both grocers focusing on natural and organic food. However, the approach varies with Sprouts adopting an indoor farmer market style general store, while Trader Joe’s is known for its private label products. The company offers organic and specialty items, generally at competitive prices. Having said that, Sprouts’ tailored selection and strong loyalty among health-focused consumers may help it hold its ground.

What’s fueling Sprouts’ Strong Run

So, what’s behind Sprouts’ strong performance and deep customer loyalty? A Pew Research Center survey found that 90% of Americans are noticing higher costs for nutritious items and 69% are say increasing food prices are making it difficult to eat healthy. It's tough for lower-income individuals, but even middle- and upper-income Americans are feeling the impact. Despite this, Sprouts Farmers Market continues its strong performance.

There is a combination of factors that has been working well for the company. Clearly, Sprouts has understood the pulse of the market, and has structured an effective strategy, embracing health and wellness. It focuses on quality and freshness in the food it offers, which is targeted towards a particular segment of people – the health enthusiasts. Sprouts actively innovates and comes up with new items monthly, as evident from the 7,100 new items it launched in 2024. The company’s key strength comes from its attribute-driven products such as gluten-free, organic, and vegan groceries, organic, non-GMO, responsibly sourced seafood / meat, plant-based dairy, cage-free eggs, and so on. Attribute-driven products drive more than 70% of the sales.

While there’s no doubt that Sprouts well-thought-out offerings and innovative assortment play a big role in driving sales, an equally important factor is the kind of audience it targets. Sprouts appeals to a slightly younger, more educated, and higher-income demographic, many of whom are in dual-income households. This makes it less vulnerable to economic downturns. These customers place a high value on healthy eating, look for fresh, organic, and less processed food. Even when consumer confidence wavers, this group’s demand remains fairly inelastic.

In addition, awareness around healthy eating is steadily rising in the U.S., giving retailers like Sprouts Farmers Market a big boost. The U.S. health and wellness food market is projected to nearly grow from $250 billion in 2023 to $469 billion by 2030. This growing demand places Sprouts in a sweet spot – its focus on natural, quality foods align perfectly with evolving consumer preferences. As more Americans prioritize health and sustainability in their diets, Sprouts is well-positioned to expand both its existing customer base and future market footprint.

While these growth drivers paint a compelling picture, it's important to examine whether the current stock price fairly reflects this momentum or if investor enthusiasm has pushed valuations too far.

Strong Performance, But Valuation Signals Caution

Sprouts' growth has been secular and appealing to investors. If we expand our timeframe from one year to two years, the share price growth will look even more irresistible – increasing four times to $165.65.

The overall financial position of the company is hale and hearty. But in terms of valuation, Sprouts trades at significantly higher valuation multiples when compared to its peers.

Sprouts’ higher price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio and forward P/E reflect strong investor confidence in its future growth potential, given the company’s consistent financial performance and expanding market presence. The elevated price-to-sales (P/S) ratio also indicates investors paying a premium for each dollar of revenue in anticipation of continued momentum in top-line growth. A higher PEG ratio also suggests that the market views Sprouts as a retailer with long-term upside.

However, this optimism appears to have pushed the stock into overvalued territory. My DCF analysis shows that the stock appears priced ~20% above its intrinsic value based on fundamental cash flow projections. I applied a 6% free cash flow (FCF) growth rate, which is below the 4-year average of 8.69%. This is to conservatively account for fluctuations in free cash flow due to rising CAPEX, with FY2024 being the only year of FCF growth. I used a 2% terminal growth rate to reflect long-term economic maturity and moderate growth expectations.

While the DCF valuation indicates the stock may already be pricey, there's another layer worth examining. The behavior of Sprouts’ CEO and leaders, who know the company best. Recent insider transactions, particularly sustained selling by Sprouts’ CEO and senior leadership, add a note of caution that investors shouldn’t ignore.

Cashing In at the Top

CEO Jack Sinclair recently sold 8,090 shares of company stock across July 7 and 8, 2025, netting approximately $1.3 million. While these transactions were executed under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan, it took place at a time when company shares have seen a sharp ascent. Sinclair sold 4,045 shares each of these two days, with average sale prices of about $162.94 and $160.63. In addition, he exercised options to buy the same number of shares at just $16.47 a unit, totaling $133,242, thus making a substantial amount of real profit.

While insider sales can stem from various personal financial decisions, the timing aligns with Sprouts’ strong market momentum. This isn’t the first time the CEO has sold shares this year. In fact, he’s been consistently offloading stock almost every month, with the exception of April. He’s not alone either, several top executives have also been selling. While insider selling doesn’t always spell trouble, a pattern of frequent sales at the top may warrant some caution. It could be a subtle sign that the stock is already trading at a premium.

Risks

So, what are the risks to consider? Sprouts’ premium valuation means even small setbacks could weigh on the stock. Slowdown in same-store sales or rising costs could pressure margins. Its focus on a niche customer base – while a strength – also makes it sensitive to changes in consumer habits or a weaker economy. Lastly, sustained insider selling may dent investor confidence if not countered by strong operating results.

Holding Steady or Jumping In? Here’s My View

Several factors could move the needle for Sprouts. On the bullish side, continued strength in comparable store sales, steady store expansion, and consistent earnings beats, particularly amid a challenging macro environment could support further upside. On the bearish side, rising competition, earnings miss, margin pressure from inflation, or regular insider selling may shake investor confidence and weigh on the stock.

I'd suggest holding your position for now. If you already own shares, there’s no reason to hit the panic button as the company’s fundamentals look solid, and it's been performing well.

But if you’re thinking about jumping in, it might be better to sit tight for a bit. The recent insider selling and multiple transaction over the year is a little out of the ordinary and worth keeping an eye on. The strength of Sprout's resilient target consumer may also be tested this year of the economy slows enough to affect this demographic. With its strong run-up this year and some macro challenges ahead, today probably doesn't offer the best entry point.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Early BDC Earnings Look Fine
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up–highlighting individual news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of July.

Market Action

It was another strong week for BDCs with the average stock in our coverage rising nearly 2%. Month-to-date, the total average return is 4%. Only GAIN is down in July so far. HRZN outperformed, jumping 14% to an above-average valuation. This one remains a puzzle given it is one of the worst performers in the sector over the last 3 years.

The sector has nearly erased its post-Liberation Day meltdown. After lightening up on the positions acquired in the first half of April, we remain on the sidelines, keeping an eye on the coming earnings season.

The average valuation in our coverage is right on top of its historic level.

However, the median valuation remains below its historical average level, indicating opportunities remain in the sector.

Market Themes

Saratoga Investment Corp (SAR) is ringing the bell for the start of the BDC earning season. The company’s earning schedule is shifted earlier by a month which allows it to be a kind of canary in the coalmine.

Overall, the quarter was good. Adjusting for the extra dividend paid because of a shift to monthly dividends, the NAV was up 0.6%. After a tough 2024 with sizable realized losses, this past quarter there was a net realized / unrealized gain. Non-accruals remained low at 0.3% of fair value while NII fell slightly, adjusted for excise tax impact.

All in all, this is a good sign for the rest of the sector where earnings and portfolios should hold up well. The impact of tariffs is expected to be limited while flat short-term rates should support NII.

Market Commentary

BDCs like MAIN, TRIN, and HRZN have been announcing their new investment activity levels over Q2. TRIN said it originated $519m of new commitments in Q2 versus $186m in Q1. MAIN originated $196m in investments versus $130m in Q1. HRZN activity, however, fell in Q1 at $60m vs. $100m in Q1. The drop in HRZN activity is not surprising as the company’s leverage is elevated at 1.29x, in part due to previous losses.

Another driver of a recovery in new lending is seasonality. Q4 tends to see a lot of activity as managers race to finish deals before year-end which saps some business from Q1, making Q2 look strong by comparison.

Yet another reason could be a recovery in M&A which has been very depressed. We will know more from manager commentary during the earnings season if deal activity has actually risen from earlier depressed levels. Net net it’s somewhat better if overall commitments are elevated as that suggests that repayments are elevated as well, meaning portfolio turnover is high. The higher it is the more BDCs can make on fees for closing out and making new loans. It also suggests that there are more deals to choose from so BDCs can be pickier in their lending.

Elsewhere there was a comment that BDCs ought to match the duration of their assets to their liabilities. This doesn’t really make sense to us - BDCs are not insurance companies, so duration matching is not an obvious requirement there.

There are almost no BDCs that match the duration of their assets to their liabilities. The duration of BDC assets is close to zero, and the duration of liabilities is something like 2-3 given the combination of fixed-rate bonds and floating-rate credit facilities.

The key risk for BDCs would be if they fail to roll over their financing, particularly their credit facilities. You are kind of seeing that with PSEC which is issuing a lot of preferreds, so even if financing becomes hard to come by that could be an option as well for other BDCs.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Financials Start Q2 '25 Earnings With A Bang; Expect Another Solid Quarter For S&P 500 Earnings
data.description:
This table has been posted a few times over the last 8 weeks, showing the progression of “expected” S&P 500 EPS and revenue growth rates for Q2 ’25.

Q2 ’25 earnings started this week, and all the large-cap financials' numbers slid: JPMorgan (JPM), Citigroup (C), Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Goldman Sachs (GS) and even American Express (AXP) this morning.

Note the darkened horizontal line on the left side of the above table: it shows that the expected Q2 ’25 EPS and revenue growth rates actually bottomed around the Memorial Day weekend, rather than the week of every quarter as is the typical pattern.

Note how with Q1 ’25 expected EPS growth rates, the Q1 ’25 EPS growth rate bottomed on April 4th ’25 (first column of data), versus how with Q2 ’25, the aforementioned bottoming of the growth rates was a full month earlier, around the end of May ’25.

Does this matter? This earnings growth rate pattern has been pretty steady for the last 15-20 years, so yes, the fact that the estimated growth rates bottomed a month earlier – in my opinion – shows a higher degree of confidence in the 2nd quarter ’25 EPS and revenue estimates.

While only 59 companies have reported Q2 ’25 results so far, (23 of the 59 companies reported are financial sector companies), the upside surprise is +7.2% and the biggest upside surprise – per sector – is financials at +10%. The revenue upside surprise for far in Q2 ’25 is +1.95%, also above average, but that figure will likely decline over the quarter.

The largest market cap weight within the financial sector in the S&P 500 is Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), which is up just 4.44% as of Thursday night, July 17th’s market close. The 2nd largest financial name in the S&P 500 by market cap weight is JPMorgan, and it was up 22.6% as of Thursday night’s market close.

Summary/conclusion

The early results for Q2 ’25 S&P 500 earnings look solid. Expect another decent quarter, although with the new August 1 deadline for tariffs, maybe the anxiety and angst now shift to the 3rd quarter of ’25. Or maybe tariffs shouldn’t have been a concern to begin with.

What’s shocking about the Q1 ’25 market drama is that the expected growth rate for the Q1 ’25 S&P 500 EPS growth rate bottomed at +7.8% in early April ’25, while the actual quarter-end growth rate peaked at +13.7%, or a 75% increase. Let’s see if Q2 ’25 estimates growth rates can beat Q1 ’25’s numbers.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IPAY: Superb Digital Payments Exposure
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome FinHeim Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Amplify Digital Payments ETF is an exchange traded fund managed by Amplify ETFs, with over $12 billion in assets across all of its ETF products and ranging different investment strategies for retail and institutional investors alike. IPAY itself has just shy of $300 million in AUM itself.

Before I dive in the details, why this ETF? And why would you invest in such an ETF when crypto and stablecoins are all the craze? There are fears that these kind of ETFs will be made redundant if stablecoins are issued by perhaps every company. Well, my thesis is that the perceived negative impact on these ETFs, especially IPAY, is overblown and that many of its holdings are poised to benefit from recent changes. This presents, in my opinion, an attractive buying opportunity.

We all know now more than ever, that cash as a transactional instrument is a dying breed, substituted by cards, online payments, mobile payments, and even crypto. Those substitutes are exactly what this ETF provides exposure to. Amplify Digital Payments ETF seeks to replicate the total return of the NASDAQ CTA Global Digital Payments Index, gaining exposure to global traditional companies in the payment space like Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA), and relatively new, disruptive companies in the industry such as crypto giant Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN), providing in my opinion the best balanced investment opportunity for retail investors.

Holdings

Going in depth, as of June 30th, 71% of the fund is comprised of large cap companies with a market cap over $10 billion. Mid-cap companies with a market cap in the range of $2-10 billion make up 25.56%, and small-cap companies represent the remaining 3.44%.

With regards to country allocation, the ETF is mostly allocated to US-based companies with over 81% of the holdings domiciled there, followed by the UK and the Netherlands coming in second and third place with 4.68% and 4.39%.

In terms of thematic allocation, as mentioned earlier, the fund brings into play a diversified set of industry players, with the bulk going to the Transaction & Payment Processing Services sector.

The fund is relatively concentrated within its top 10 holdings, with over 50% of the NAV attributed to those. the fund holdings numbering 37 as of July 15th. The expense ratio is 0.75%, somewhat higher than other thematic ETFs.

The Industry

Let’s first look at the market this ETF addresses. According to Fortune Business Insights, the global mobile payment market is valued at $3.84 trillion in 2024 and is forecasted to grow to $26.53 trillion by 2032, forecasting a staggering CAGR of 27%. Naturally having the highest population, the Asia Pacific region dominated the global market with a share of 45.31% in 2024. The increased coverage of high-speed mobile networks and the increase in smartphone usage are the main catalysts behind this kind of projected growth.

North America is the second largest market, with already developed and plentiful payment methods, attracting consumers through different strategies such as payback and reward systems. Europe as a market is traditional as in majority of the people use bank accounts and payment cards, and as such represents a big potential addressable market for innovative payment solutions. I have already seen such developments with partnerships between banks and payment providers boosting mobile based transactions. Further evidence of potential is the strategic decision made by PayPal Holdings Inc. (PYPL) to introduce near field communication (NFC) cards in the UK and Germany, among other European markets.

Moving on the dark cloud for the industry over the last few months: the idea that stablecoins can hamper the growth of some established companies in this industry. This worry led to a large dent in the ETF's performance this year relative to the broader market.

In the last couple of months, crypto and specifically stablecoins come to the forefront of investors’ attention through the GENIUS Act and, notably, the report last month that Walmart and Amazon, two of the biggest merchants are exploring how to issue stablecoins, potentially impacting high volumes of payments outside traditional financial system and affecting the payment ecosystem.

The news had an immediate impact and soured investor sentiment towards many payment giants, with Visa Inc. (V), Mastercard, PayPal and Block, Inc. (XYZ) falling sharply. This kind of exuberated reaction encompasses the jittery investor sentiment towards the industry in recent years. My opinion is that although stablecoins and crypto are the technology of the future, they pose a limited threat to the digital payment giants.

Healthy Perspective Is Warranted

Firstly, as previously mentioned, the traditional card system is just too practical to disrupt entirely. With consumers latching onto rewards such as cashbacks, they just don’t have the necessary incentives to change their behavior. I would like to also note that the major credit card companies and payment processors have already been building stablecoin capabilities, with PayPal pioneering the charge with the launch of PYUSD stablecoin in 2023. Most recently, Fiserv launched its own stablecoin, with the intention of helping its regional and community client banks preserve their market share.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, what I like about this ETF is that there are many companies that haven’t touched their 2021 highs even though revenue has risen quite a lot since (I acknowledge that the pandemic boom incorporated high expected growth, but in my opinion, the long term potential of this industry hasn’t lost its shine). This represents fresh gunpowder for years to come.

What’s notable is that all of the top 10 companies have double digit net profit margins and relatively lower ratios compared to their average in the early 2020’s. Traditional companies in the space such as Visa, Mastercard and American Express Company (AXP) provide the stability, while newer companies such as Coinbase, Block, Affirm Holdings Inc. (AFRM), Toast, Inc. (TOST) and others are expected to shape the future of payments.

Another aspect to consider is that the P/E of the fund is around 23x, while the S&P500 is at around 27x. I believe that in the near future, market sentiment will improve and will favor a convergence towards the market multiple, giving me short-term target of $70.

Risks

The risks to my thesis are that stablecoins capture a chunk of the market short-term, which would be visible in the near-term. As a result, transaction volumes may reduce for some of IPAY's largest holdings, compressing margins.

Also, a drop in interest rates would hurt margins for some of the larger, traditional companies. On the other hand, this could also have a positive effect regarding the discount rate and resulting valuation. Long term, the investment risk is slower adoption of digital payments, impacted by security concerns.

Conclusion

The IPAY Amplify Digital Payments ETF presents an attractive investment opportunity for the long term, offering exposure to the digital payments industry through traditional companies and growth companies. The current investor’s sentiment is overly negative and should improve as the clouds clear in the next few quarters regarding stablecoin adoption. Currently, this presents a good entry point.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Inches Up In July
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment inched up in July, increasing for a second straight month. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index was up 1.8% (1.1 points) to 61.8 this month. This is the index's highest level since February but still reflects historically low levels of optimism. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 6.9% (4.6 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment was little changed from June, inching up about one index point to 61.8. While sentiment reached its highest value in five months, it remains a substantial 16% below December 2024 and is well below its historical average. Short-run business conditions improved about 8%, whereas expected personal finances fell back about 4%. Consumers are unlikely to regain their confidence in the economy unless they feel assured that inflation is unlikely to worsen, for example if trade policy stabilizes for the foreseeable future. At this time, the interviews reveal little evidence that other policy developments, including the recent passage of the tax and spending bill, moved the needle much on consumer sentiment.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 61.8, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 26.8% below its average reading of 84.4 (arithmetic mean) and 25.8% below its geometric mean of 83.3, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 6th percentile of the 571 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 1.1-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In July, the CECI rose to 66.8, its second consecutive monthly increase and the highest level since January. This represents a 3.1% increase from the previous month and a 6.5% rise from a year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 63.9.

Meanwhile, the CEI rose to 58.6, its third consecutive monthly increase and the highest level since February. This represents a 0.9% increase from the prior month and a 14.8% decline from one year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 55.0.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations fell for a second straight month, plunging from 5.0% last month to 4.4% this month. Long-run inflation expectations receded for the third consecutive month, falling back from 4.0% in June to 3.6% in July. Both readings are the lowest since February 2025 but remain above December 2024, indicating that consumers still perceive substantial risk that inflation will increase in the future.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: OTC Markets: Cash Flow And Dividends
data.description:
OTC Markets (OTCQX:OTCM) is the perfect example of a small-cap company that has compounded earnings over a long time period who is severely underfollowed (2,900 followers on SA).

I've written about the organization in the past, and you can find that here. The thesis is the same, but some positive data was released this first half of the year which trends the overall organization's business in a positive direction. Late January of 2025 OTC Markets released an announcement that total dollar volume traded across its markets increased approximately 25% compared to previous year totals. This underpins my thesis on the company that it plays a pivotal role in providing a lower cost solution for smaller and international companies looking for liquidity and access to investors.

OTC's profit pools all serve a purpose for unique trading and information capabilities. The company continues to expand offerings to inform investors and bring companies options for liquidity and capital raising.

  • OTC Links - Smallest revenue segment. Operates ATS (alternative trading system) which allows brokers and dealers to efficiently provide the best execution and choice of counterparties on trades while complying with SEC rules.
  • Market Data Licensing - This segment distributes market data and financial information. Also offers services for compliance laws through the Blue Sky Data product. In addition, they've acquired EDGAR Online expanding OTC's data information and compliance offerings to customers.
  • Corporate Services - This is the exchange segment operating OTCQX and OTCQB. OTCQX is for publicly traded companies wanting exposure to investors at a lower cost and complexity standpoint. OTCQB is more for venture markets.

Moat and Business Model

The ability to drive high returns on invested capital shows the company has some qualities investors should look at. ROIC and ROE both provide a data point in a similar way. How much cash does the company generate based on its invested capital and debt structure? Well, OTC does a pretty good job at this.

Return on equity 75% and return on total capital 42% both show a business generate strong capital returns. Comparing these metrics to other high-quality companies OTC is in the upper quartile of returns while being less known.

The business model is centered around providing data to investors, connecting buyers and sellers, and managing public markets where they connect businesses to investors. All of these services are capital light once the infrastructure is in place. Low capital expenditures are a cash flow statements bestfriend and OTC is no stranger to this. They've roughly only spent $1 million a year on Capex the last few years for an org that's valued at $675 million and generates $26 million in free cash flow.

With being capital light the business has a high margin. Incremental transactions within their ecosystem of products allow margin expansion. Providing data and connecting buyers and sellers require little additional cost with each additional customer.

Network effects surround the ecosystem of products OTC offers. Connecting buyers and sellers and managing a stock exchange allows both sides of the networks to grow as they execute making the product offerings stronger. As more investors trade on their exchanges more businesses will want to list on such exchange.

Cross selling is another advantage the organization has as they repackage data they gather from their own stock exchange and sell it through licensing agreements. A wonderful feedback loop is a result of these offerings.

The business has the potential to compound capital for years to come. The company is resilient with some pathways for growth. Foreign companies that want access to US markets OTC Markets is one of the only offerings for this service at a reasonable price. In addition, the company has no debt, generates strong cash flow, and even on the liability side of the balance sheet is minimal.

Valuation

The valuation has expanded since I last wrote about when the cash flow yield was above 5%, now it sits at 3.9%. The cash flow yield calculation is simple but powerful data point that truly can indicate undervaluation through comparison and market knowledge. For instance, high-quality companies over the years have traded between 1.5% to 5% free cash flow yield. With that knowledge when quality organizations get closer to the upper quartile of the range undervaluation could be occurring. Investors would need to study current driving factors of a downward trend in value but is a hint of undervaluation. OTCM is not moving in that direction, and that stock price has gotten a little more rich.

The last three months have been good for investors increasing by almost 25%. The increase has put the valuation in the middle of our understanding of high quality business valuation. At the moment I would rate the company a hold to a small buy based on valuation. Pushing 4% yield isn't bad just not extreme undervaluation.

In addition, I wanted to point out the company does return cash mainly through dividends. Over the last 3 years the company has paid out $43 million in dividends equating to 6% of the current market cap.

Risks

Economic conditions in the business world do effect OCTM as during more difficult times for business they will postpone or not list as they are in survival mode. Long-term this isn't an issue as historically business markets have grown which OCTM should be able to continue to capture.

Regulation risk is real for OTCM as they operate in a highly regulated market protecting investors. OCTM has been around for decades to navigate regulations and policies which makes a barrier around the business as competitors will have to follow also. Any new entrances would have to learn how to navigate these rules.

Final Thoughts

With extremely high returns on capital, potential for growth, and cash being returned to investors through dividends this should be a business to watch for any quality investor. The co. isn't talked about enough in my opinion as they have network effects, feedback loops across multiple product offerings, and are already generating strong cash flow. The balance sheet is also a fortress for the co. with only $47 million of total liabilities for a company that sells for $675 million. The company is currently valued by market participates with a reasonable price making it a hold to a small starter position purchase.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: High Profit And Low Debt Could Transform Headwater Exploration Into The Next Oil Star
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Headwater Exploration (OTCPK:CDDRF) (TSX:HWX:CA) a Strong Buy rating. I think that the company can increase its revenues and earnings in the upcoming years. The first aspect is its high EBIT margin of 47.58%, considerably higher than the margins reported by its peers. As such, the company can use its revenues to expand its operations. In addition, it anticipates producing 26,500 barrels of oil equivalent ((boe/d)) in 2027, representing a 30.48% increase from 2024. This year, it expects to pay a dividend yield of 6.35%. Another relevant aspect is that Headwater has a low debt level. For instance, in Q1 2025, it reported a 25.22% liabilities-to-assets ratio. Furthermore, its management team has been successful in the oil industry.

The WTI forward curve suggests lower oil prices for the upcoming years. This curve indicates that the WTI spot price could drop to values in the range of $59.7 to $63.3 per barrel in the upcoming years. If this scenario occurs, my estimation is that the operating margin of Headwater could fall to 40.13%. On the other hand, the OPEC+ expects to add more than 2 million barrels of oil this year, thereby increasing the global oil supply. With more oil in the market, the oil prices could decline, negatively affecting Headwater's revenues. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of Headwater, I still consider the company to be an interesting investment opportunity.

The Company

Headwater is an oil and gas company headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada. In Q1 2025, its heavy oil production accounted for 88% of the total output, while natural gas and liquefied natural gas represented the remaining 12%, as depicted in the following chart. Its business strategy focuses on assets that generate attractive long-term returns while maintaining low debt levels.

History

The transformation of Headwater began in 2020. That year, the company restructured its management team. In addition, it acquired the assets of Cenovus (CVE) located in the Marten Hills area of Alberta, Canada. Those assets consisted of producing 2,800 boe/d of heavy oil that year. For this transaction, Headwater paid a total consideration of approximately CA$135.3 million. The same year, it changed its management team. Thanks to these decisions, Headwater increased its production from 620 boe/d in 2019 to 7,393 boe/d in 2021. To provide context, Martens Hills averaged 6,785 boe/d in 2021. That year, Headwater invested CA$140 million in capital expenditures.

Moving to 2022, Headwater expanded its operations to Greater Peavine and West Nipisi, located in Alberta, Canada. In those places, it acquired CA$33 million of land. Due to these acquisitions and the significant investments in Capex, Headwater's production reached an average of 22,066 boe/d in Q1 2025.

Headwater achieved this result by issuing common shares. In 2019, it had 88,147 common shares, increasing to 237,774 in Q1 2025, representing a 169.75% increase. Further, the company has employed its own resources to expand its operations. In 2024, it reported an operating profit of CA$242.18 million and a capex of CA$133.82 million. These figures indicate that Headwater has significant resources to expand its operations.

Low Debt

Debt is an important aspect to evaluate in any company. There are companies with excellent growth performance, but they finish in bankruptcy due to their high debt. As such, I think that analyzing Headwater's debt will enhance the analysis presented in this article.

In Q1 2025, Headwater reported cash and cash equivalents of CA$125.55 million and total current assets of CA$187.48 million. Meanwhile, in the same quarter, it had current liabilities of CA$115.38 million and non-current liabilities of CA$128.65 million, as depicted in the following chart. In other words, Headwater can pay its current liabilities and part of its non-current liabilities only using its cash and cash equivalents. Furthermore, the company had a 25.22% liabilities-to-assets ratio in Q1 2025. This ratio indicates that the company has low debt levels.

Headwater has significant financial resources to meet its obligations. As such, even if oil prices decline, the company will have resources to pay its liabilities. Ultimately, this solid financial position reduces the risk of bankruptcy. With a low bankruptcy risk, Headwater's stock price is less likely to experience a sharp decline. In other words, there is more upside potential than downside risk.

Profits

Headwater has reported significant net profit margins, as depicted in the chart below. Since 2020, its net income margin has been above 28%. In the first quarter of 2025, the WTI oil price averaged $71.78 per barrel, 7.38% lower than its average in the same quarter of 2024. Despite this decline, the company achieved a high net income margin of 36.81% that quarter.

This section would be incomplete without comparing Headwater with other Canadian oil and gas companies. The following table shows some relevant profit ratios of Headwater, Baytex Energy (BTE), ARC Resources (OTCPK:AETUF), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), CVE, Ovintiv (OVV), Strathcona Resources (OTCPK:STHRF), Vermilion Energy (VET), Suncor Energy (SU), Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), and Bonterra Energy (OTCPK:BNEFF). I selected a large list of companies to highlight that Headwater has the highest profitability ratios. For instance, it has a 76.44% gross profit margin, followed by BTE, which has a 69.07% gross profit margin. Additionally, its EBIT margin is 47.58%, significantly higher than the values reported by its peers. Finally, the return on equity of Headwater is almost three times the median value of the peer group. Due to these excellent ratios, the company has achieved outstanding financial results.

Management

In 2020, Headwater underwent a management team change, which significantly transformed its operations. There are two key figures in this new team. The first is its executive chairman, Neil Roszell, and the second is its CEO, Jason Jaskela. Both were founders of Raging River Exploration and Wild Stream Exploration.

In the Sedar, I found that Ranging River produced 5,665 boe/d in 2013, increasing to 22,867 boe/d in 2017. Furthermore, Ranger River achieved net income margins of 8.67% and 14.91% in 2016 and 2017, respectively. The company was profitable despite the fact that the WTI oil price averaged $43.29 per barrel in 2016 and $50.80 per barrel in 2017. For instance, in 2024, the WTI crude oil price averaged $76.63 per barrel. I include these numbers to highlight that Headwater's CEO and executive chairman have a proven track record of success in managing oil companies.

Outlook and Revenues

For 2025, the company anticipates a 10% increase in its production. With that increase, it will produce 22,250 boe/d, as depicted in the following chart. To reach this goal, Headwater plans to invest CA$225 million in capital expenditures. In addition, it forecasts to produce 26,500 boe/d in 2027, up 30.48% more compared to 2024.

To enhance the analysis, the following table shows the revenue and EBITDA growth rates of Headwater, BTE, AETUF, CNQ, CVE, OVV, STHRF, VET, SU, WCPRF, OBE, and BNEFF. These companies are relevant oil and natural gas producers in Canada. In the last five years, Headwater reported a CAGR of 150.31% in its revenues, the highest growth by a large margin among its peers. Meanwhile, its EBITDA grew at 24.70% over a three-year period, also the highest value among its peers. To conclude, the company has reported higher growth metrics than its peers.

Dividends

Headwater also pays dividends, which is a positive aspect. This year, the analyst consensus expects a dividend yield of 6.35%, the second-highest value among its peers, as depicted in the following chart. In addition, it paid an average dividend yield of 3.27% in the last four years.

Valuation

In this section, I use the relative valuation method to estimate the fair value per share of Headwater. For this purpose, I implement the relative valuation technique to the following ratios: price-to-earnings, EV/EBITDA, and price-to-book ratio. The companies to use in the analysis are BTE, AETUF, CNQ, CVE, OVV, STHRF, VET, SU, and WCPRF. With these firms, I estimate the median value of each ratio.

To begin with, I use the median as a target ratio. When implementing the relative valuation technique to the price-to-earnings ratio, the fair value of Headwater is $6.94 per share, as depicted in the table below. At that price, Headwater is undervalued by 37.67%. Meanwhile, for the EV/EBITDA ratio, the relative valuation method suggests that $5.56 is the fair value per share of Headwater, pointing out that the company is undervalued by 10.29%. Finally, the relative valuation applied to the price-to-book ratio suggests that Headwater is overvalued by 48.30%. This is the only ratio that suggests an overvaluation.

Up to this point in the article, Headwater has paid more dividends. In addition, it has higher profit ratios than its peers. Two out of three relative valuations indicate that the company is undervalued compared to its peers. I believe that these findings are signs that Headwater presents a significant investment opportunity. As Warren Buffett said:

It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price.

I think that Headwater ascribes to the previous quote. The relative valuation techniques implemented in this section suggest that the company is slightly undervalued. Headwater is also a wonderful business with an excellent profit margin and a fast growth rate.

Price Analysis

The following chart illustrates that Headwater has experienced sharp declines in its stock price in different periods. In addition, from 2007 to 2019, its stock price lost most of its value. I am of the opinion that the high volatility that the company has experienced still affects Headwater. Many investors may be deterred from investing in Headwater due to its poor previous performance.

However, the company has radically changed since 2020. For instance, in 2018, its operations were concentrated on extracting natural gas. Meanwhile, in 2024, its operations were primarily focused on oil extraction. Furthermore, the company has a new management team. These factors lead me to believe that if oil prices do not experience a sharp decline, the company's stock price will not experience a drastic drop in the upcoming years.

Reserves

I think that Headwater has low oil and gas reserve levels compared to other Canadian companies, which is a negative aspect. In 2024, Headwater had a proven reserve life index of 4.7, as depicted in the table below. In other words, at the production level of 2024, its proved reserves will last for 4.7 years. Furthermore, the company had proved and probable reserves of 7.4 years. For instance, in 2024, CNQ said that it has reserves for 33 years and proved plus probable reserves for 43 years. Additionally, VET had 9 years of proved reserves and 14 years of proved plus probable reserves in 2024.

Oil Outlook and Operating Profit Margin

The oil market expects lower prices for the upcoming years. In my experience analyzing the commodity market, the forward curve is a useful tool for gaining insight into the price of a particular commodity over the month and year ahead. For instance, the WTI future price of this month trades at $66.37 per barrel, as depicted in the chart below. Meanwhile, the WTI future price of May 2026 is $62.43 per barrel. As such, from now to May 2026, the WTI barrel of oil is expected to decline by $3.94. On the other hand, the forward curve chart indicates that the WTI oil prices will range from $59.7 per barrel to $63.3 per barrel between 2027 and 2036. Based on that range, I think that $61 per barrel is a reasonable assumption as the long-term price of this commodity for the upcoming years.

With the previous chart and Headwater's financial information, it is possible to derive the company's expected operating margin. In the following table, I calculated Headwater's operating costs per boe, highlighted in red. For example, in 2024, the operating costs to produce a barrel of oil were $27.19, while the WTI oil price averaged $76.63 per barrel. I used the annual average exchange rate between the Canadian dollar and the U.S. dollar to transform the operating costs to U.S. dollars. Hypothetically, if Headwater had sold its boe production at the WTI oil price, it would have reported an operating profit margin of 65.24% in 2024. If the WTI price declines to $61, this metric will decline to 55.43%, representing a 15.04% decrease compared to the value of 2024.

Turning to Headwater, the last column of the table displays the company's actual operating profit margin. If I assume that Headwater's operating profit margin declines by 15.04%, it will fall to 40.13%. This value represents the operating profit margin that Headwater may achieve if the WTI price declines to $61 per barrel, which is the expected long-term WTI price.

OPEC+

On July 5, 2025, OPEC+ agreed to increase its oil production by 548,000 barrels per day. For this year, the OPEC+ plans to increase its production by 1.918 million barrels of oil. Additionally, the oil organization anticipates adding another 580,000 barrels of oil per day this year. As such, this year the global oil supply will experience a significant increase, which could push oil prices down. If this occurs, Headwater's revenues will be negatively affected.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider Headwater to be an excellent investment opportunity. To begin with, the company has a management team that has been successful in the oil industry. In addition, it has low debt levels, which indicates that it can resist a period of low oil prices. It also has a high EBIT margin of 47.58%, significantly higher than those of its peers. Furthermore, it expects to produce 26,500 boe/d in 2027, an increase of 30.48% compared to 2024. The company also expects to pay 6.35% in dividends this year, another positive aspect.

Headwater has low levels of proven reserves, which is a negative factor. It has proved reserves for 4.7 years. To provide context, CNQ has proved reserves for 33 years. Further, the WTI forward curve suggests lower oil prices in the upcoming years. My estimation suggests that if the WTI oil price declines to $61 per barrel, the operating expenses of the companies could decline by 15.04%. Additionally, the OPEC+ expects to add more than 2 million barrels of oil to the market this year. The significant increase in the oil supply could push oil prices down. When taking all of these factors into consideration, I still believe that Headwater represents an interesting investment opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Companhia Siderurgica Nacional: The Deleveraging Asymmetry Play
data.description:
Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional (NYSE:SID), also known under the acronym "CSN", is one of the largest steel producers in Brazil, as well as the second largest iron ore producer, behind the giant Vale SA (VALE) in the country.

The company's investment thesis is based on its capital-intensive model and dictated by its sensitivity to the prices of the commodities it exports, as well as a high exposure to FX. In recent years, the company has suffered from the fall in iron ore prices and a gradual but slow recovery in steel prices, very pressured by uncertainties over the ongoing tariff war. The unfavorable flow of investment into emerging markets such as Brazil has also hurt. On top of this, CSN has suffered from high leverage and negative free cash flows—points I highlighted in my previous coverage, where I adopted a neutral stance on the ADR.

That said, with performance virtually flat throughout this year, at least stemming the bloodletting that took place in 2024 when SID devalued by more than 60%, I believe that the results posted in 1Q25 may be signaling a deleveraging cycle that could prove very pricey for CSN over the coming months.

Although the projections for iron ore prices are still not necessarily encouraging for the course of this year, in an eventual scenario of interest rate cuts in the U.S. and potentially in Brazil, with CSN doing its homework in favor of efficiency control and deleveraging, the path tends to be towards a reduction in interest expenses and falling leverage, making room for free cash flow generation.

Trading at EBITDA multiples almost 50% below its global peers, and well below its book value, although the discount is justified by the high country risk involved in the thesis, I believe that this discrepancy tends to fall sharply in a scenario of deleveraging, even if it is gradual. That's why I see an asymmetry in CSN's thesis, and I'm upgrading my rating from Hold to Buy—going against the sell-side consensus.

Commodity Cycle and Currency Impact on CSN

First of all, CSN is a highly cyclical company, since it operates in steel and mining (mainly iron ore), which are directly linked to global economic cycles. Because of this, the performance of its ADR tends to have a high correlation with two factors that I see as key being (1) commodity prices, especially iron ore, steel, and their derivatives; and (2) FX based on being an exporting company from Brazil, if the USD rises, this helps revenues in BRL.

The graph below shows that, today, SID has a correlation of 0.85 to iron ore spot prices, which denotes a very high sensitivity to commodity swings—albeit with some ups and downs in this correlation over the last twelve months. For example, in the last twelve months, SID has plummeted from $2.40 to $1.44, in similar proportions as iron ore has fallen from $108 USD/t to ~$92 USD/t.

In terms of steel, the HRC steel price at $875 USD/t has shown a certain stability throughout this year, after strong rises between Jan/Mar 2025 (from ~700 to ~950). Although CSN's main driver is iron ore, HRC remains an indirect proxy as the company sells flat steel in Brazil and also exports. And the firm HRC price guarantees healthy margins.

The last but not least point is with regard to FX, as today 1 USD equals 5.58 BRL—despite a peak of 6.40BRL earlier this year. Specifically for CSN, the weaker BRL is, in theory, advantageous, as exports tend to yield more in BRL, from where the company's costs originate.

But as CSN's ADR is traded in USD, if the BRL weakens too much, market risk sentiment tends to worsen, and so foreign investors price more risk into Brazil as a whole—risk-free rates are high, and capital flows out. However, with the macro scenario showing a slight perception of lower risk over the course of this year—as shown by Brazilian equities rallying in 2025—the inversely proportional correlation has been reversed over the past few months.

So, I would argue that CSN has spent the last twelve months in a situation of double pressure (1) with iron ore falling ~15% in that period and (2) with the USD falling since the peak, reducing the "natural hedge" of exports. The counterpart is HRC steel, holding up the price.

The market has penalized CSN's ADR more than iron ore alone would justify. So, I see part of the sharpest fall in this period as extra risk premiums based on company-related issues such as (1) high leverage, and (2) competition in the domestic market.

CSN Recent Developments

After a clearer view of the macroeconomic factors affecting CSN, its 1Q25 results have been choppy QoQ but actually decent YoY.

Starting with the high points, CSN is coming off record mining sales, up 5.4% year on year, still demonstrating solid demand, reflected in revenue 2.1% higher than the same period last year, despite iron ore prices still under strong pressure. Mining EBITDA advanced by 26% year-over-year, with a margin of 41%, reaching R$1.4 billion at a more efficient C1 Cost of $21/t, which is 11% lower than the same period last year.

As steel prices have appreciated over the last twelve months, steel sales grew by 8% year-over-year, causing the segment's EBITDA to more than double to R$485 million, with margins of 7.9% versus 4.3% in 1Q24.

The other less significant segments, cement, logistics, and energy, boosted the consolidated EBITDA result to R$2.5 billion, with a margin of 22%, an increase of 28% compared to the same period in 2024.

The problem is that in the quarterly comparison, EBITDA came in 24.8% lower, most notably dragged down by mining, steel, and cement, which came in R$571 million, R$171 million, and R$145 million lower, respectively.

That said, it was not possible to convert this amount into free cash flow.

Subtracting from the R$2.5 billion in EBITDA, R$1.12 billion in CapEx, and a further R$1.2 billion in financial results (due to high leverage), along with income tax expenses and others, the adjusted free cash flow was negative at R$173 million.

The bright spot is that, at least, based on the bottom line improvements year-over-year, CSN still managed to reduce its gross debt by R$3.6 billion, which helped bring its gearing from previously 3.5x in 4Q24 to 3.3x in 1Q24.

What’s Next for CSN?

The expectation is that, over the course of 2025, just as it proved in 1Q25, the deleveraging path will continue more robustly and may converge in CSN's favor.

Because CSN is a capital-intensive company, in the event of a change in interest rates, leverage here tends to be an asset. At the moment, CSN's debt structure is ~66% in USD (with an average cost of 6.60% per year) and ~34% in BRL (tied to 117% of the CDI, i.e., a massive 17.4%).

In other words, the ideal mix to accelerate leverage here would be the Fed cutting interest rates + the Brazilian Central Bank starting to lower interest rates (Selic) by 15%. The trend in July 2025 is for cuts, not hikes, with the consensus for Fed Funds leaning towards 2-3 gradual cuts by the end of 2025. In Brazil, the cut bias is also a reality, but potentially slower and more limited, with Selic projections closing 2025 at the same 15%. So, I would say that the odds are very much in favor, in the current situation, of CSN deleveraging further over the course of 2025.

But of course, the scenario is not so simple. The commodities environment remains hostile, with trade wars likely to weigh on maintaining downward pressure on iron ore. Platts TSI 62% Fe CFR China has been priced as a consensus of between R$95 and $110/t for the year ended December 31, expecting a slight recovery in H2 if there are new stimuli in China. However, no one is betting on a return to $130-150/t, as in previous cycles.

As for the cost of extraction, C1/t, CSN, which historically has a higher cost than Vale SA—because the mines are not as large in scale, but are improving efficiency—from $20-$23/t versus $18-$20/t. But CSN's projections for 2025 remain at between ~$21/t to ~$23/t, and, in my view, it is unlikely to lower the cost more than that without expanding scale.

That said, I at least see CSN on the right track, doing its part, which is (1) looking for ways to deleverage in the face of monetary tightening and unfavorable commodity cycles; (2) diversifying its operations into logistics, energy, and diluting sectoral risks; (3) leveraging favorable exchange rates, while still being able to benefit from an appreciated USD against the BRL, as well as strict exchange rate and financial hedge control to protect margins and cash.

And then there's the question of dividends. As CSN's shareholder remuneration policy is flexible, the company also distributes dividends based on accumulated profit reserves, even when there are periods (such as the current one) when net profit is negative. For example, last year, in November, CSN approved the distribution of R$730 million in interim dividends, using its more than R$24 billion in cash reserves (at that time), which resulted in an average yield of 14.3% in 2024.

Over the course of 2025 alone, a further R$1.3 billion in dividends have been announced and are expected to be distributed by the end of the year, which today results in a yield of ~16%—quite high but also a reflection of the sharply falling share price over the last twelve months.

The management team has shown an interest in balancing the distribution of dividends with the need to reduce leverage, and that, as the company progresses in this area, the plan is to return to levels closer to R$1 billion per semester.

Of course, it's prudent to see these robust dividends as something potentially temporary, rather than a constant and secure flow. But it also seems quite attractive as leverage gives way to a reduction in the financial result (interest expenses), weighing on cash flows, and as the company returns to posting a positive free cash flow with greater stability.

A Quick Word Ahead of 2Q25 Earnings

That said, the next acid test for CSN is imminent. The company is preparing to report its 2Q25 earnings on July 31, and to beat market estimates, it will need to report EPS above $0.11—that is, a good improvement on the -$0.07 reported in 2Q24 and the -$0.10 reported in 1Q25. Revenues will need to come in above $1.99 billion, which would represent virtually no improvement on the same period last year, although it is slightly above the $1.93 billion reported last quarter.

According to management commentary, in the last earnings call, the commitment to further debt reduction was strongly emphasized, and that, with EBITDA cooperating, could bring a good chance of improvement in leverage ratios as early as 2Q25.

And although there was no explicit guidance for mining, the implication is that it is expected that in Q2, seasonality/weather would normalize, so record volumes would not be repeated. Therefore, the focus would be on cost discipline (C1 cost control), which doesn't change much from 1Q25.

Regarding steel, CSN commented that it expects to maintain the solid performance in the segment after raising prices in 1Q25 and mentioned that stable costs helped double EBITDA. However, I wouldn't be surprised if competitive dynamics (especially in long steel) could put pressure on margins versus last quarter. But overall, my view is for a sustained EBITDA quarter-over-quarter for steel.

Finally, with regard to the cement segment, the trend is for a recovery in 2Q25 driven by seasonality, pushing EBITDA close to historical levels again—this could be a pleasant surprise in consolidated EBITDA, resonating well with the deleveraging process.

All in all, the odds are against 2Q25 being a "super" quarter. I'd expect more of the same from 1Q25, but with year-over-year EBITDA growth, which should help post more exciting leverage metrics.

Risks and Final Remarks

Trading at 4.2x its forward EBITDA, and below its book value at 0.83x, it seems that the pessimism of the commodities cycle added to all the pressures brought by leverage seems at least to be embedded in the share price.

However, as I pointed out at the beginning of the article, the performance of CSN's ADR is highly correlated with iron ore prices, steel industry demands, and FX, potentially offsetting any operational or financial improvements made by CSN's management team.

But overall, it seems to me that, at penny stock levels, Companhia Siderurgica Nacional is somewhat de-risked in the face of a cycle that is becoming more and more conducive to deleveraging, and a virtual stability in commodity prices for the coming months. Defying the prevailing market view, which is largely bearish on Brazilian iron ore and steel companies, I see in SID a point of asymmetry for those who tolerate volatility and understand that the biggest risk—the leveraged balance sheet—can be gradually mitigated as CSN prioritizes deleveraging and adjusts its asset mix to reinforce cash.
